---
title: "Yang-Mills Lattice Gauge Theory - William's Explorations"

bibliography: references.bib

# format:
#   html:
#     code-fold: true
# jupyter: python3
---

::: {.hidden}
$$
\newcommand\mcy{{\mathcal{Y}}}
\newcommand{\R}{\mathbb{R}}
\newcommand{\Tr}{\text{Tr}}
\def\*#1{\mathbf{#1}}
$$
:::

## May 25, 2026

### Thesis Workflow

I'll use Uni. Bonn's .tex file for the title page
of my thesis. Since I don't want to bother with integrating
their tex conventions into my own, I'll just render the title page
as a pdf, then insert those two title pages to my final submission. 

I'll make a git repository with my latex code. 

I'll have a common preamble amongst all the separate chapters.

I'm using [this guy's](https://www.ravisagar.in/notes/how-get-directory-tree-structure-your-mac-terminal)
advice for getting a display of the folder structure.

See also [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/23989232/is-there-a-way-to-represent-a-directory-tree-in-a-github-readme-md).

```bash
thesis-test
├── chapter1
│   ├── chapter1.aux
│   └── chapter1.tex
├── chapter2
│   ├── chapter2.aux
│   └── chapter2.tex
├── main.aux
├── main.bbl
├── main.bcf
├── main.blg
├── main.log
├── main.out
├── main.pdf
├── main.run.xml
├── main.synctex.gz
├── main.tex
└── references.bib
```

### Tex to Quarto

I'll explain how to convert a latex project involving .tex and
.bib files to a quarto website rendition. 

First you create your latex project on your local machine. 
Let's say you have a <script.tex> file and a <references.bib> file, 
and a bunch of other files excreted during pdf rendering.

Second, you make sure that your quarto website folder 
has a <references.bib> file that includes all the entries in 
the latex project's <references.bib> file. 

Then you go to the website <https://pandoc.org/app/>. 
You upload your <script.tex> document into the engine. 
Then you click on <latex (LaTeX)> as the <From:> option
and you choose <markdown (Pandoc)> as the <To:> option. 
Then you click convert and you save the resulting <script.md>
file to your quarto website project. 

Then you just rename the file <script.md> to <script.qmd>
and then proceed as usual in quarto.

### Discussion of some definitions

<details>

<summary>lattice gauge theory on $\epsilon \mathbb Z^d$ </summary>

Fix $\epsilon > 0$. 
We note $\epsilon \mathbb Z = \{ a \in \mathbb R: a = \epsilon k
\text{ for some } k \in \mathbb Z\}$. Then 
$\epsilon \mathbb Z^d = \{(a_1, \cdots, a_d) \in \mathbb R^d: a_i \in \epsilon \mathbb Z \}$.
We say two vertices $x,y \in \epsilon \mathbb Z^d$ are adjacent 
if $x = y \pm \epsilon e_i$ for some $i \in [d]$. In this case 
we write $x \sim y$.
Let $\Lambda \subset \epsilon \mathbb Z^d$ be a finite subset. 
Let $E_{\Lambda} = \{(x,y) \in \Lambda^2: x \sim y \text{ and } x < y \}$, 
where $x < y$ means that $\frac{1}{\epsilon} x < \frac{1}{\epsilon} y$ in the 
lexicographical ordering.
A configuration is a map $U: E_\Lambda \to U(N)$, where we also
define $U(y,x) = U(x,y)^*$ for $(x,y) \in E_\Lambda$.

</details>

<details>

<summary> Density </summary>

We follow section 7.4 of @Klenke. 
Let $\mu$ and $\nu$ be measures on a measurable space $(\Omega, \mathcal{A})$.
A measurable function $f: \Omega \to [0, \infty)$ is called a density of 
$\nu$ with respect to $\mu$, if 
$$
\nu(A) := \int_{\Omega} f 1_A \, d\mu
$$
for all $A \in \mathcal{A}$.
On the other hand, for a given measurable function $f: \Omega \to [0,\infty)$,
the above equation defines a measure $\nu$ on $(\Omega, \mathcal{A})$.
In this second case, we write 
$$
\nu =: f\mu, \hspace{1 cm} f =: \frac{d\nu}{d\mu}.
$$

Given a measurable $g: \Omega \to [0,\infty]$, 
integrating against $d\nu$ is the same as integrating against
$f d\mu$. This is theorem 4.15 @Klenke: 
Let $f \mu$ be the measure defined above. Then $g \in \mathcal{L}^1(f \mu)$
if and only if $(gf) \in \mathcal{L}^1(\mu)$. In this case, 
$$
\int g \, d(f\mu) = \int gf \, d\mu.
$$

There is also a uniqueness result, Theorem 7.29.
Let $\nu$ be $\sigma$-finite. Let $f_1$ and $f_2$ be two densities
of $\nu$ with respect to $\mu$. Then $f_1 = f_2$ $\mu$-almost surely.
In particular, the density $\frac{d\nu}{d\mu}$ is unique $\mu$-almost surely.
That is, there is a $\mu$-null set $N \in \mathcal{A}$ such that 
$\frac{d\nu}{d\mu}$ is equal to any other given density function on the complement
$\Omega \setminus N$. (Not sure if I stated this uniqueness correctly)

</details>

<details>

<summary> Lebesgue Measure </summary>

The following is Theorem 1.55 of @Klenke. 
There exists a uniquely defined measure $\lambda^n$ on 
$(\mathbb R^n,\mathcal{B}(\mathbb R^n))$ with the property that
$\lambda^n((a,b]) = \prod_{i=1}^n (b_i - a_i)$
for all $a,b \in \mathbb R^n$ with $a< b$. Furthermore, $\lambda^n$ is called
Lebesgue measure on $(\mathbb R^n, \mathcal{B}(\mathbb R^n))$
or Lebesgue-Borel-measure.

</details>

<details>

<summary> Gaussian measure on $\mathbb R^n$ </summary>

Fix the Lebesgue measure space 
$(\mathbb R^n, \mathcal{B}(\mathbb R^n),dx)$.
Let $\tau$ be a probability measure on 
$(\mathbb R^n, \mathcal{B}(\mathbb R^n))$.

More logically rigorous is to say that
$\tau$ is a Gaussian measure if
there exists a constant $C> 0$ and a degree two polynomial
$P: \mathbb R^n \to \mathbb R$ such that
$Ce^{-P(\cdot)}: \mathbb R^n \to [0,\infty)$ is a density of 
$\tau$ with respect to $dx$:
$$
\tau(A) = \int_{\mathbb R^n} Ce^{-P(x)} 1_A \, dx
$$
for all $A \in \mathcal{B}(\mathbb R^n).$

In this case we write
$$
\frac{d\tau}{dx} = Ce^{-P(x)}.
$$

Suppose $\tau$ is a Gaussian measure with polynomial $P$.
Suppose $Ce^{-P(\cdot)}$ is not integrable. Then by definition of 
integrability (Def 4.7 @Klenke, for example), it follows that 
$\int_{\mathbb R^n} |Ce^{-P(x)}|dx = \infty,$
which contradicts that $\tau$ is a probability measure on $\mathbb R^n$.
Hence, $\tau$ Gaussian implies that $Ce^{-P(\cdot)}$ is integrable.

Since $C$ is a finite constant, $Ce^{-P(\cdot)}$ is integrable if and 
only if $e^{-P(x)}$ is integrable.

Lemma (Criterion 12.1 @Chatterjee2016). The measurable function $e^{-P(\cdot)}$
is integrable if and only if 
there exists a positive constant $c$ and a positive constant $R$ such that
$$
P(x) \ge c \lVert x \rVert^2
$$
for all $x \in \mathbb R^n$ satisfying $\lVert x \rVert \ge R$, 
where $\lVert x \rVert = \sqrt{x_1^2 + \cdots + x_n^2}$.

Lemma. If $P: \mathbb R^n \to \mathbb R$ is an arbitrary degree two 
polynomial, then there exists a matrix $Q \in \mathbb R^{n \times n}$,
a column vector $v \in \mathbb R^n$, and a constant $c \in \mathbb R$
(not the same $c$ as above) such that
$$
P(x) = x^T Q x + v^Tx + c.
$$

Lemma (Criterion 12.2 @Chatterjee2016). Suppose 
$P(x) = x^T Q x + v^Tx + c$. Then
the following are equivalent.

- $e^{-P(x)}$ is integrable.
- There are positive constants $C_1, R$ such that 
$$
P(x) \ge C_1 \lVert x \rVert^2
$$
for all $\lVert x \rVert \ge R$.
- $Q$ is a positive definite matrix. That is, $Q$ is symmetric
and $x^TQx > 0$ for all $x \ne 0 \in \mathbb R^n$.

</details>

<details>

<summary> Positive Definite Matrix </summary>

Consider the $\mathbb R$ vector space 
$\mathbb R^n$. Consider a symmetric 
$\mathbb R$-bilinear form
$g: \mathbb R^n \times \mathbb R^n \to \mathbb R.$
Recall that symmetry means
$g(x,y) = g(y,x)$ for all $x,y \in \mathbb R^n$.
We say that $g$ is positive definite if 
$0 < g(X,X) =: X^2$ for all $X \ne 0$ in $\mathbb R^n$ 
(@LangAlgebra Section Symmetric Forms over Ordered Fields).
Let $Q$ be the matrix associated with the form $g$
relative to the standard basis 
$B = \{e_1, \cdots, e_n\}$, that is, 
if $x, y \in \mathbb R^n$, and in this case
$x$ and $y$ are already the column vectors of coordinates
for themselves with respect to the standard basis,
then
$$
g(x,y) = x^T Q y.
$$
(See Section Matrices and Bilinear Forms @LangAlgebra.)
In this case we say that $Q$ is a positive definite matrix.

Note also that $Q$ is implicitly a symmetric matrix, by the
following result.

Proposition 6.5 (Section Matrices and Bilinear Forms @LangAlgebra).
Let $E$ be a free module of dimension $n$ over a commutative ring $R$,
and let $B$ be a fixed basis. The map $f \mapsto M_B^B(f)$
induces an isomorphism between the module of symmetric bilinear forms
on $E \times E$ and the module of symmetric $n \times n$ matrices
over $R$.

In summary, $Q$ is a positive definite $n \times n$ matrix
if and only if $Q$ has entries in $\mathbb R$, $Q = Q^T$ (symmetry), 
and $x^TQx > 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb R^n$ not equal to $0$.

</details>


## May 13, 2026

In constrast to the notion of Lebesgue measure on $T_N^{(1)}$ used
above, the following notion uses an isometry between
$T_N^{(1)}$ equipped with Hilbert Schmidt norm and $\R^{2N-1}$ equipped
with the Euclidean norm.

*Definition.* Alternative Lebesgue Measure on $T_N^{(1)}$.
In this definition, we view $T_N^{(1)}$ as an $\R$-vector space endowed with
the Hilbert Schmidt norm $\lVert M \rVert = \sqrt{\Tr(M^TM)}$, which, by the way, is induced
by the scalar product $(A,B) = \Tr(A^TB).$ 
We consider the topology on $T_N^{(1)}$ generated by 
open balls $b(Y, r) = \{X \in T_N^{(1)}: \lVert X - Y \rVert < r\}$, 
namely the topology induced by the Hilbert Schmidt norm.
Then we consider the Borel sigma algebra on $T_N^{(1)}$
which is generated by the open sets in the topology above.
We also view $\R^{2N-1}$ as an 
$\R$-vector space with the Euclidean norm $\Vert x \rVert = \sqrt{\sum_{i} x_i^2}$.
There is an isometry 
$$
\Phi: T_N^{(1)} \to \R^{2N-1} 
$$
given by
$$
T_N^{(1)} \ni \begin{bmatrix}
a_N & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}b_{N-1} & &  &  \\
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} b_{N-1} & a_{N-1} & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} b_{N-2} &  & \\
 & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} b_{N-2} & & & \\
 & & \ddots& \ddots & \\
 & & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} b_2& a_2&\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} b_1 \\
 & & & \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} b_1& a_1
\end{bmatrix} = T \mapsto (a,b) \in \mathbb R^{2N-1}.
$$
Fix $T \in T_N^{(1)}$ as above. 
Then 
\begin{align*}
\lVert T \rVert^2 &= \Tr(T^TT) = \Tr(T^2) \\
&= \left(a_N^2 + \left(\frac{b_{N-1}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2\right) + \left(\left(\frac{b_{N-1}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + a_{N-1}^2 + \left(\frac{b_{N-2}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2\right) + \cdots \\ 
&= \sum_{i=1}^N a_i^2 + 2 \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} b_i^2/2\\
&= \lVert (a,b) \rVert^2 = \lVert \Phi(T) \rVert^2,
\end{align*}
which shows that $\Phi$ is an isometry, 
and that the $\sqrt{2}^{-1}$ factor was needed
to preserve the isometric correspondence.
We define Lebesgue measure on $T_N^{(1)}$, which we denote by $l_{T_N}$, such that
$l_{T_N} = \Phi^* (\mathcal{L}_{\R^{2N-1}})$, the 
pullback of $2N-1$ dimensional Lebesgue measure
by the map $\Phi$. That is, 
for a Borel subset $B \in \mathcal{B}(T_N^{(1)})$, we have
$$
l_{T_N}(B) = \mathcal{L}_{\R^{2N-1}}(\Phi(B)).
$$ 
In addition, we note that 
the pushforward of $l_{T_N}$ by $\Phi$, denoted by 
$\Phi_* l_{T_N},$ is equal to $\mathcal{L}_{\R^{2N-1}}.$

*End Definition*

## May 4, 2026

[Here](https://drive.google.com/drive/u/0/folders/1utG5S-e3Nzdyet0UWugVWob7riQ1aqAc)
are some notes on the Frenet frame field of a curve. 
We follow page 56 of O'Neills book on Elementary Differential Geometry.
The idea is to take together the vecolity of the curve, 
the acceleration, and the cross product of the acceleration
and velocity to get a frame vector field.

## May 1, 2026

Here is a report on an internet search from this morning.
My initial goal was to look for applications
of what I'm learning in @Dimitriu2018. 
Therefore I looked on Google Scholar to see who
had cited that paper. 
The first one that caught my eye 
was titled ["Tridiagonalizing random matrices"](https://journals.aps.org/prd/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevD.107.126001) 
written by Balasubramanian, Magan, and Wu, published in 
2023 in Phy Rev Let D. Some of the computations
in the paper looked familiar. 
At least one the authors was associated with the
[Sante Fe Institute](https://www.santafe.edu/), which I had
never heard of before. This institute
doesn't look relevant for me as a Master student, aside
from the two research assistants they hired, who 
are Master/Bachelor level. For the most part, it looks
like it is geared towards post-docs, but at least it is promising
that there are places outside academia for post-docs. 

Next I clicked on the article, also citing Dimitriu and Edelman's article,
titled ["Eigenvectors from eigenvalues: A survey of a basic identity in
linear algebra"](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1908.03795) 
written by 
Denton, Parke, Tao, and Zhang. I was surprised to see 
in an article on linear algebra that
Denton, Parke, and Zhang were members of physics associations. 
Denton, for instance, was a member of 
[Brookhaven National Lab](https://www.bnl.gov/careers/) in Upton, NY.
I didn't find any perfect matches
for my background of a Master in Math and Bachelor
in Math and CS, but I think there must be something.
But the point is not so much to find a job, but
to see that there is hope of finding something outside
of corporate finance and corporate tech which 
still uses technical skills, especially mathematics. 
There was also [Fermi Forward](https://www.fermi-forward.org/)
which is in Illinois. This lab is connected with U. Chicago, which
is not relevant to my background, but is also connected to 
U. Illinois Urbana Champaign, which might be relevant to me. 

Another thing I learned is that there 
is collaboration between national labs and 
PhD candidates. For instance, [here](https://internships.fnal.gov/science-graduate-research-program-scgsr/).

Perhaps more insightful is to look at, 
for instance, the career path of [Denton](https://peterdenton.github.io/). 

#### Other notes

I don't understand "And in
any case, from the unitary invariance of GUE, we see that even after
conditioning on $D$, we may assume without loss of generality that $U$
is drawn from the invariant Haar measure on $U(n)$. In particular, $U$
and $D$ can be taken to be independent" on page 219 of Tao's Random matrix book.

Update: he [responded](https://terrytao.wordpress.com/2010/02/23/254a-notes-6-gaussian-ensembles/comment-page-1/#comment-692464).
